content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

September 26, 1995

Marc Strassman
Transmedia Communications
15150 Dickens Street
Suite 105
Sherman Oaks, California 91403-3350

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-95-291

Dear Mr. Strassman:

We respond to your request for advice regarding the
contribution definition under the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1 As your question is general in nature and does not
relate any specific set of facts, we have deemed it to be a
request for informal assistance. (Regulation 18329(c).)

You have requested advice in determining whether
technological assistance would constitute a reportable
contribution. Your examples include the donation of electronic
equipment on a temporary basis, to a candidate or committee, to
assist in the propagation of the campaign's political message or
the gathering of signatures via the Internet.

Under Commission Regulation 18215(b)(3), a contribution
includes any goods or services received by or of the benefit of a
candidate or committee at no charge or at a discount from the fair
market value, unless the discount is given in the regular course
of business to members of the public. Accordingly, as a general
rule, the provision of computer programming services to a ballot
measure committee for the purpose of establishing home pages on
the Internet would result in reportable contributions.
Additionally, any equipment or professional services provided to
the committee would likewise result in a reportable contribution.

A proponent's obligation to report expenditures in support of
a ballot proposal begins when the proposal becomes a measure. (In

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Sections
18109-18995.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. I-95-291
Page 2


re Fontana (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 25.) Initiatives, referendums and
recall elections become measures at the time petitions begin
circulating to place the issue on the ballot. (Section 82043.)
Proposals which require the action of an elected body to be placed
on the ballot do not become measures until the appropriate body
has placed the proposal on the ballot. (Martini Advice Letter,
No. A-93-378.)

     We trust that you find this response helpful.  Should you
have any additional questions please do not hesitate to contact
the Commission.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel
                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Daniel E. Muallem
                                             Counsel, Legal Division


SGC:DEM:ak

sections.question: You have requested advice in determining whether technological assistance would constitute a reportable contribution. Your examples include the donation of electronic equipment on a temporary basis, to a candidate or committee, to assist in the propagation of the campaign's political message or the gathering of signatures via the Internet.
sections.conclusion: Under Commission Regulation 18215(b)(3), a contribution includes any goods or services received by or at the behest of a candidate or committee at no charge or at a discount from the fair market value, unless the discount is given in the regular course of business to members of the public. Accordingly, as a general rule, the provision of computer programming services to a ballot measure committee for the purpose of establishing home pages on the Internet would result in reportable contributions. Additionally, any equipment or professional services provided to the committee would likewise result in a reportable contribution.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the provision of technological assistance, including temporary donation of electronic equipment and computer programming services to candidates or committees, constitute a reportable contribution under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Technological assistance, including computer programming services and equipment donations provided at no charge or below fair market value to candidates or committees, constitutes a reportable contribution under the Political Reform Act unless the discount is given in the regular course of business. The obligation to report such contributions begins when a proposal becomes a measure.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter. Clear question and conclusion sections present. File number 1-95-291 confirms 1995 date. Document discusses contribution definition under Political Reform Act with specific application to Internet and technology-related assistance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have requested advice in determining whether technological assistance would constitute a reportable contribution. Your examples include the donation of electronic equipment on a temporary basis, to a candidate or committee, to assist in the propagation of the campaign's political message or the gathering of signatures via the Internet.

CONCLUSION: Under Commission Regulation 18215(b)(3), a contribution includes any goods or services received by or at the behest of a candidate or committee at no charge or at a discount from the fair market value, unless the discount is given in the regular course of business to members of the public. Accordingly, as a general rule, the provision of computer programming services to a ballot measure committee for the purpose of establishing home pages on the Internet would result in reportable contributions. Additionally, any equipment or professional services provided to the committee would likewise result in a reportable contribution.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that technological assistance such as computer programming services and equipment donations to candidates or ballot measure committees constitute reportable contributions unless provided in the regular course of business at standard discounts. The reporting obligation begins when a proposal becomes a measure, with different rules for initiatives, referendums, and proposals requiring elected body approval.
